request.question: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?

title: Saw Delight
The Last Great Traffic Jam
Steve Winwood
When the Eagle Flies
Rosko Gee
Probka
Traffic wave
Gold (Traffic album)
China National Highway 110 traffic jam
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi
sentences: Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.
The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.
The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.
Stephen Lawrence "Steve" Winwood (born 12 May 1948) is an English musician whose genres include rock, blue-eyed soul, rhythm and blues, blues rock, pop rock, and jazz.
Though primarily a vocalist and keyboardist, Winwood also plays bass guitar, drums, acoustic and electric guitar, mandolin, violin, and other strings.
When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.
It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".
The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.
(Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)
The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".
He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.
He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
"Probka" (Russian Cyrillic: Пробка; English translation: Traffic Jam) is a song by Latvian singer Intars Busulis, and was the Latvian entry at the Eurovision Song Contest 2009 in Moscow, Russia.
The song competed in the second semi-final of the contest on 14 May 2009 but failed to reach the final, having finished last with 7 points.
The song is unusually rhythmically complex for a Eurovision song, with the main verse section played in the unusual time signature of 7/4, and constant changes in tempo.
Busulis described "Probka" as "a song about a traffic jam in all senses and meanings.
We all have experienced in our lives that we just hurry, rush somewhere and at the certain moment there is a stop, jam or "probka".
That’s it – there is no way out, we are stuck!
This is an appropriate moment to think if it was worth it to run and hurry at all."
Traffic waves, also called stop waves or traffic shocks, are traveling disturbances in the distribution of cars on a highway.
Traffic waves travel backwards relative to the cars themselves.
Relative to a fixed spot on the road the wave can move with, or against the traffic, or even be stationary (when the waves moves away from the traffic with exactly the same speed as the traffic).
Traffic waves are a type of traffic jam.
A deeper understanding of traffic waves is a goal of the physical study of traffic flow, in which traffic itself can often be seen using techniques similar to those used in fluid dynamics.
Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.
It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.
The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.
Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.
It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi is a 2017 Indian Mizo-language documentary film narrated, produced and directed by C. Hmingthangvunga.
The film tells about the traffic jam in Aizawl, the capital of Mizoram State in India.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?
hop1_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

Far from Home (Traffic album) | Far From Home, released in 1994, is the eighth and final studio album by the rock band Traffic, and the first in two decades since the release of "When the Eagle Flies" in 1974.  It was recorded at a large house called Woodstock, outside Kilcoole to the south of Dublin, and mixed at the Chateau Miraval in Correns, southern France.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album) | To Whom It May Concern is a 1972 album by the Bee Gees.  Released in October 1972, it was the follow-up, and continued the melancholic and personal sound of its predecessor "Trafalgar".  The album was recognized as "a farewell to the old Bee Gees" as the album marked the end of an era for the group in several ways: it was their last album to be recorded at IBC Studios, in London, their last with conductor and arranger Bill Shepherd who had guided them since 1967, and their last under their first contract with Robert Stigwood.  Some of the songs were old ones finished up or rewritten for the occasion (in the case of "I Can Bring Love").  "To Whom It May Concern" has sold approximately 350,000 copies worldwide.
hop1_titles: To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album)
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Rosko Gee — [1]: "has played with the English band Traffic on their albums"  
- Rosko Gee — [2]: "Rosko Gee on bass guitar;"  
- "When the Eagle Flies" — [2]: "When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album"  
- "(1974)" — [1]: "(1974)"  
- "The Last Great Traffic Jam" — [3]: "The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD"  
- "(2005)" — [1]: "(2005);"

Ambiguities:
- None for the band identity: "English band Traffic" is consistently named.  
- Minor personnel note: [3] describes "The Last Great Traffic Jam" as a 1994 reunion live album but does not list performers, while [1] asserts Rosko Gee played on that 2005 release.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing relation for the question: the passages explicitly state Rosko Gee played with the English band Traffic on those albums.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Rosko Gee" (to confirm biography/credits)  
- Page: "Traffic (band)" (to confirm band personnel and album credits)  
- Page: "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (Traffic album) (to verify live album personnel/credits)  
- Query: "Rosko Gee When the Eagle Flies personnel" or "Rosko Gee The Last Great Traffic Jam credits"  

Final conclusion: Rosko Gee played with the English band Traffic on those albums. [[ ## completed ## ]]

He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
Rosko (disambiguation) | Rosko is a New York singer and music producer.

William (Rosko) Mercer | William Roscoe Mercer (1927-2000), better known to millions of radio listeners simply as Rosko, was an American news announcer and disc jockey.  He is best known for his stints on New York's WOR-FM and WNEW-FM in the 1960s.  Rosko and other DJs of the time pioneered alternative-format FM radio, in response to the restrictive playlist programming of Top 40 AM stations.

Emily Rosko | Emily Rosko (born 1979) is an American poet and is on the faculty of the College of Charleston.  She is the author of "Raw Goods Inventory" (2006) and "Prop Rockery" (2012) poetry collections, both of which have won awards.

Emperor Rosko | Michael Joseph "Mike" Pasternak (born 26 December 1942), known by his stage name Emperor Rosko, is an American presenter of pop music programmes, most widely known for his shows on Radio Caroline and BBC Radio 1 in the UK in the 1960s and early 1970s.
response.summary_2: Rosko Gee played with the English band Traffic on the albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005).
